perturbation.original_text: In the event Zulu receives any solicitation, proposal or offer with regard to the foregoing, Zulu shall provide written notice to Swansi within two business days of its receipt by Zulu or its principals.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event Zulu receives any solicitation, proposal or offer with regard to the foregoing, Zulu shall provide written notice to Swansi within five business days of its receipt by Zulu or its principals.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the notification period to five business days may violate securities regulations that require prompt disclosure of material events.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not explicitly dictating a 2-day notification, delaying the notification of a material offer for five days may not constitute the prompt and timely disclosure required under SEC Rule 10b-5, particularly if the information is deemed material to investors. Rule 10b-5 prohibits acts that operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person in connection with the purchase or sale of any security. Delaying notification could potentially be construed as misleading if it withholds material information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the notification period from two business days to five, which may violate securities regulations requiring prompt disclosure of material events. The Law Explanation suggests that delaying notification of a material offer for five days might not constitute timely disclosure under SEC Rule 10b-5, especially if the information is deemed material to investors. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 17 CFR § 240.10b-5, prohibits acts that operate as a fraud or deceit, including omitting a material fact necessary to prevent statements from being misleading. Delaying the notification, even for a few days, could be construed as withholding material information and thus contradict the law.

perturbation.original_text: Each party will use the Confidential Information it receives solely for the purpose of evaluating the Acquisition and not for any other purpose and, except to the extent permitted by paragraph 1.5 hereof, will keep such Confidential Information strictly confidential, provided, however, that Confidential Information may be disclosed to such Representatives as needed to know such information for the purpose of evaluating and negotiating the terms of the Acquisition and for no other purpose.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party will use the Confidential Information it receives solely for the purpose of evaluating the Acquisition and not for any other purpose and, except to the extent permitted by paragraph 1.5 hereof, will keep such Confidential Information strictly confidential, provided, however, that Confidential Information may be disclosed to such Representatives as needed to know such information for the purpose of evaluating and negotiating the terms of the Acquisition and for any other purpose.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'for no other purpose' clause violates confidentiality standards and possibly trade secret laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: By allowing disclosure of Confidential Information for 'any other purpose,' the changed text contradicts the intent of protecting trade secrets. The Colorado Uniform Trade Secrets Act (C.R.S. § 7-74-102) provides remedies against the misappropriation of trade secrets. This modification would create a significant loophole, undermining the protections afforded by the Act, as it removes the restriction on using the information solely for the Acquisition.

perturbation.justification: The original text limited the use of confidential information to 'evaluating and negotiating the terms of the Acquisition and for no other purpose.' The changed text removes the 'for no other purpose' clause, directly contradicting the intention to protect confidentiality. The Law Explanation highlights that removing this restriction undermines the protections afforded by trade secret laws, as it creates a loophole allowing the information to be used for any purpose. The original clause, by limiting the use, provided a clearer safeguard. The modified text removes that safeguard.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that the parties hereto do not proceed with the Acquisition by October 31, 2007, and, in any event, within five (5) days after being so requested by either party, both parties shall return or destroy all documents furnished by the other.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that the parties hereto do not proceed with the Acquisition by October 31, 2007, and, in any event, within thirty (30) days after being so requested by either party, both parties shall return or destroy all documents furnished by the other.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the return deadline to thirty days could violate data protection laws if the information includes Personally Identifiable Information (PII) and regulations require faster deletion or return.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the Colorado Consumer Data Protection Act (CCPA) doesn't specify a strict deadline for returning or destroying data upon request within a confidentiality agreement, the law emphasizes data minimization and reasonable security measures. Retaining data for 30 days after a request, especially if it includes PII, could be seen as excessive and not adhering to the principle of data minimization, especially if a shorter retention period is more reasonable and technically feasible.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ication, data minimization, avoiding secondary use, care, avoiding unlawful discrimination, and sensitive data; Requires controllers to conduct a data protection assessment for each of their processing activities involving personal data that present a heightened risk of harm to consumers, such as processing for purposes of targeted advertising, profiling, selling personal data, or processing sensitive data; and Specifies that a violation of its requirements is a deceptive trade practice for purposes of enforcement, but the act may be enforced only by the attorney general or district attorneys. Local governments are preempted from adopting laws that govern the processing of personal data by controllers or processors. The attorney general may promulgate rules to administer the act and is required to adopt rules detailing technical specifications for a universal opt-out mechanism that controllers must use. (Note: This summary applies to this bill as enacted.) perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: may lead to misuse of Coloradans’ personal information. Also, the notice must provide certain information that could help consumers protect themselves against identity theft. If you believe that a company has violated your rights or failed to fulfill an obligation under the CPA or data breach law, please file a complaint below. Tips on Submitting a Complaint The more information you can provide about the situation, the more useful your report will be. If possible, be prepared to provide the following information: What is your issue about? Be clear and concise. Briefly describe your issue and what steps you have taken to resolve it. Be sure to include relevant dates and other details about your report, including , if possible, any CPA consumer right you feel may have been violated, or CPA obligation that was not fulfilled. Information about the company or seller If available, please be prepared to provide information on the business or individual you are reporting on, such as entity name, address, phone number, website, email address, and any other identifying information. Your contact information Please be prepared to provide your contact information, such as your name, address, phone number, and your email address. Why file a complaint Every complaint we receive gives us insights into problems that people are experiencing in Colorado and helps us identify trends and prioritize problems. Information from the public is vital in helping us document those trends and, when appropriate, pursue investigations and file lawsuits on behalf of the public. Please note: The Attorney General’s Office does not have authority to provide legal advice or legal representation to individuals and does not have the authority to investigate or prosecute your individual case. File a Complaint Contact Us Contact the Attorney General’s Office to report a complaint about a business or if you have questions or comments. Questions or Comments The Colorado Privacy Law goes into effect on July 1, 2023. If you would like to file a complaint based on the Colorado Consumer Protection Act, please click here . Get on the mailing list If you wish to receive updates on informational meetings, provide future rulemaking input, or otherwise be involved with the rulemaking process, please click the button and complete the form to receive updates and notices: INFORMATIONAL & RULEMAKING MEETING NOTICE SIGN-UP → You can also receive additional notices about this rulemaking and other Colorado rulemakings by completing the form on the Department of Regulatory Agencies webpage .
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the data return deadline from five to thirty days. The Law Explanation says that retaining PII data for 30 days after a request 'could be seen as excessive and not adhering to the principle of data minimization'. Scraped Law Snippet 1 supports this, stating that the Colorado Consumer Data Protection Act (CCPA) emphasizes 'data minimization' and 'reasonable security measures'. This suggests the changed text, by allowing a longer retention period, introduces a contradiction because it potentially violates the data minimization principle.

perturbation.original_text: Zulu and its principal shareholders agree that until October 31, 2007, or such shorter period if either party notifies the other that it no longer wishes to proceed with the Acquisition (“Term of this Agreement”), that they will not, directly or indirectly, solicit, initiate or encourage submission of proposals of offers from any third party relating to any acquisition, purchase or option to purchase an equity interest in Zulu, or any merger, consolidation or business combination with Zulu or the sale of substantially all of the assets of Zulu.
perturbation.changed_text: Zulu and its principal shareholders agree that until October 31, 2008, or such shorter period if either party notifies the other that it no longer wishes to proceed with the Acquisition (“Term of this Agreement”), that they will not, directly or indirectly, solicit, initiate or encourage submission of proposals of offers from any third party relating to any acquisition, purchase or option to purchase an equity interest in Zulu, or any merger, consolidation or business combination with Zulu or the sale of substantially all of the assets of Zulu.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the standstill period to a year may violate antitrust laws if it unduly restricts competition.
perturbation.law_explanation: Extending the standstill agreement to a full year, without proper justification, could potentially violate Section 1 of the Sherman Antitrust Act (15 U.S.C. § 1). This section prohibits contracts, combinations, and conspiracies in restraint of trade. While standstill agreements are not per se illegal, a year-long restriction could be considered an unreasonable restraint of trade if it unduly prevents Zulu from exploring other acquisition opportunities and limits competition in the market, especially if there isn't a clear business justification for such a long duration.

perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the standstill period from October 31, 2007, to October 31, 2008. This modification, as explained, could potentially violate Section 1 of the Sherman Antitrust Act (15 U.S.C. § 1) by creating an unreasonable restraint of trade. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both highlight that this extended period, if without proper justification, limits competition and Zulu's opportunities, supporting the existence of a legal contradiction. The original text had a shorter, potentially more reasonable timeframe, while the changed text significantly increases this period. Snippet 1 mentions the Sherman Act and its codification in 15 U.S.C. § 1, which supports the LLM explanation.
